# 8 Warning Signs It’s Time to Resurface Your Pool

Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua.

On Posted on [March 27, 2025March 27, 2025](https://alansmithpools.com/warning-signs-its-time-to-resurface-your-pool/) by [Jeff Smith](https://alansmithpools.com/author/jeffsmith/) to [Pool Tips](https://alansmithpools.com/category/blog/)

## 8 Warning Signs It’s Time to Resurface Your Pool

Pool resurfacing is a crucial step for homeowners who want to maintain a comfortable and attractive swimming environment. Over time, even a well-maintained surface can develop cracks, rough textures, and discoloration that impact both safety and visual appeal. To learn how experts address these problems, explore our detailed [pool resurfacing process](https://alansmithpools.com/pool-resurfacing-process/) for a clearer picture of each stage.

Whether you’ve had your pool for a few years or a few decades, it’s important to know when repairs or resurfacing might be needed. This article highlights why timely resurfacing is beneficial, then reveals eight common warning signs that indicate it’s time to renew your pool’s surface.

## Why Resurfacing Matters

Resurfacing does more than upgrade your pool’s look. It can help prevent serious structural issues, improve swimmer comfort, and minimize the need for constant upkeep. In fact, homes with recently resurfaced pools tend to sell for about 7% more than similar properties with older pools, showing just how much a fresh finish can boost value.

Equally important, a high-quality finish stands up to chemical imbalances and seasonal temperature changes. If you’d like to learn about broader renovation aspects such as tile replacement or equipment upgrades, check out our [A Homeowner’s Guide to Pool Remodeling](https://alansmithpools.com/a-homeowners-guide-to-pool-remodeling/) for additional insight.

## Sign \#1: Peeling or Flaking Plaster

Peeling plaster, also called “spalling,” appears when thin layers of the pool’s surface start flaking off. This often shows up on steps or the pool floor and usually stems from low pH or consistently imbalanced water chemistry. Compared to stains or minor roughness, this form of deterioration signals advanced damage that cannot be reversed without new surface material.

Once plaster begins to peel, the underlying structure is more vulnerable to further damage. While a small stain might only be a cosmetic annoyance, peeling plaster indicates significant surface breakdown that calls for prompt attention.

## Sign \#2: Persistent Stains

Regular scrubbing and minor chemical adjustments can remove many stains. However, if you see discoloration that keeps coming back—often bluish, greenish, or brownish in color—this may indicate that the finish itself is compromised. Mineral deposits, including copper or calcium, sometimes bond deeply with older surfaces and resist standard cleaning techniques.

Though superficial stains might be less severe than a plaster problem, they still disrupt the pool’s overall look. Persistent stains can also hint at underlying factors like unbalanced water, which might damage other areas more rapidly than you’d expect.

## Sign \#3: Rough Textures

A pool floor or wall that feels abrasive can be a pain for swimmers and a sure sign that resurfacing is on the horizon. Worn plaster or aggregate becomes coarse due to chemical erosion or long-term water friction. In fact, rough areas are found in up to 78% of pools requiring resurfacing (Alan Smith Pools, n.d.).

While roughness might seem less threatening than flaking plaster, it often leads to skin scrapes and accelerates overall surface breakdown. Once abrasive spots begin spreading, a full resurfacing is typically more cost-effective than recurring patch jobs.

## Sign \#4: Visible Cracks

Cracks in a pool can range from superficial hairline fractures to more serious structural issues. Hairline cracks might only degrade the finish, but deeper cracks risk leaks and shell damage. In many regions with freezing temperatures or ground movement, these cracks can worsen and spread quickly.

Compared to a minor stain or slight roughness, cracks pose a bigger threat since they may allow water to seep behind the pool’s shell. If you discover noticeable cracks, resurfacing combined with professional repair is the safest approach to protect your investment.

## Sign \#5: Swimmer Discomfort or Injuries

When swimmers complain about scratched feet or small abrasions, your pool surface likely needs attention. Irritations often happen if worn plaster leaves a sandpaper-like texture or if chipped edges appear around cracks. A smooth finish not only feels better but also reduces the chance of water chemistry imbalances, because there are fewer crevices for algae and minerals to settle in.

If injuries have become more frequent—like cuts on toes or elbows—this sign is more pressing than just discoloration. At that point, taking action is vital for both safety and the pool’s structural health.

## Sign \#6: Significant Leaks

A gradual drop in your water line can indicate normal evaporation or minor splashout. However, if you must constantly refill your pool to stay at the correct level, leaks could be forming through cracks, aged plaster, or deteriorated seals. Untreated leaks often harm the pool’s foundation and can even affect surrounding deck areas.

Leaks tend to be one of the bigger red flags compared to surface stains or mild roughness. Addressing them quickly with certified repairs and new finishes keeps long-term repair expenses down and protects the entire property from water damage.

## Sign \#7: Outdated or Discolored Finishes

Colors that look faded and finishes that appear decades old can make your pool less inviting. Sun exposure and harsh chemicals cause many surfaces to lose their original luster. In some cases, the finish can become mottled, resulting in uneven color patches.

Though an outdated look might not be as urgent as a structural crack, letting it go often overlaps with more hidden issues. If your surface is old enough that the color is completely worn down, consider selecting a modern finish by visiting our [Selecting a Surface: Pool Surfaces](https://alansmithpools.com/pool-resurfacing-and-replastering/selecting-a-surface-pool-surfaces/) resource to find attractive, long-lasting options.

## Sign \#8: Excessive Chemical Use

Maintaining correct chemical levels plays a major role in your pool’s durability. If you notice you’re using significantly more chemicals than usual to keep the water balanced, it might be because the aging surface is leaching minerals or reacting poorly to standard treatments. According to Cognitive Market Research (2023), improper water chemistry accounts for up to 50% of premature surface failures.

Needing more chlorine, pH balancers, or algaecides over time also implies that cracks and tiny holes exist where algae or bacteria might hide. When chemical adjustments stop being enough, it’s best to consider a new surface that’s less porous and more resistant to water penetration.

## Choosing the Right Resurfacing Material

When it’s time to tackle any of these warning signs, selecting the correct finish sets the stage for a longer-lasting pool. Common materials such as plaster, pebble, and quartz differ in both cost and durability. Plaster can be budget-friendly but may require resurfacing more often, whereas pebble or quartz finishes offer a higher initial cost but a significantly extended lifespan.

Environmental conditions matter, too. Areas with frequent temperature shifts may call for surfaces that are resistant to cracking, while sunny climates might benefit from finishes designed to withstand UV rays. Balancing cost, style, and longevity often leads to the best outcome for your backyard retreat.

## Wrapping Up

Monitoring your pool for peeling plaster, stains, rough textures, cracks, leaks, and other signs of damage helps you stay ahead of major repairs. Addressing these issues before they spiral into structural failures can save on costs and preserve your investment in the long run. If you’re ready to take the next step, feel free to [contact our team for a professional assessment](https://alansmithpools.com/contact-us/) and enjoy a worry-free swimming experience.

With a well-planned resurfacing strategy, your pool can remain a source of relaxation, fun, and pride for many years.

## References

# Time to Resurface the Pool? 8 Signs to Look For

[![Resurface the pool signs](https://www.gpspoolstore.com/wp-content/uploads/resurface-the-pool-signs.jpg)](https://www.gpspoolstore.com/signs-to-resurface-the-pool/)

September 21, 2022[Pool Remodeling](https://www.gpspoolstore.com/category/pool-remodeling/)Glenn Simpson

People tend to take a lot of pride in their swimming pools. They really bring the overall visual aesthetic of someone’s yard together, not to mention the fact that they’re a great place to host friends and family members for gatherings.

But as soon as a pool starts to look rundown (or even just begins to show its age), all of that is called into question. The pool may no longer make that excellent first impression it was once known for.

When that day comes, resurfacing the pool will likely be in order. But what is the difference between a problem that can easily be corrected on its own and a situation that calls for resurfacing the pool as soon as possible?

**Related Article:** [Resurfacing a Pool: The Process and What to Expect](https://www.gpspoolstore.com/resurfacing-a-pool-process/)

## Signs You Might Need to Resurface the Pool

You can easily make this determination by watching out for a few of the bigger signs along the way.

### Pay Attention to Stains

![Pool stains](https://gpspoolstore.com/wp-content/uploads/pool-stains.jpg)One sign that it may be time to resurface the pool becomes evident when certain types of stains develop.

Generally, even stubborn pool stains should clean or brush out in a fairly straightforward manner. You may have to use a little force, but it will be doable. However, if you are dealing with the types of stains that will not go away regardless of how hard you try, this could be a clear sign that a resurface job is in your future.

In general, stains happen when debris like leaves are not cleared from the bottom of your pool on a regular basis. Likewise, algae can cause stains if left unchecked for a long period. Skipping regular maintenance or neglecting to maintain the right balance of chemicals in the water can also lead to issues.

Regardless of the reason, stains that won’t brush out are a sign that you will need to resurface the pool sooner rather than later.

### The Water Level Keeps Dropping

Typically, the water level in your pool shouldn’t change very much regularly. This is true regardless of how often it’s being used and how many people are splashing around. If your water level keeps dropping for no immediately obvious reason, it could be a sign of a far more severe problem.

The most likely cause is that you have a leak somewhere in or around your pool. If the leak is present on the side or bottom of the pool, resurfacing would certainly be a way to fix it. To determine if this is the case, use a [pool leak detection kit](https://www.gpspoolstore.com/pool-leak-detection-kits-do-they-work/) (or even some food coloring or a similar colored liquid) in the water in the area where you think a leak may exist. The colored fluid should move toward the leak like a current.

If it doesn’t, the leak may be present in the underground plumbing that you have around your pool. In this context, you wouldn’t need to resurface the pool, but you would still have to call in a professional to help address the issue.

**Related Article:** [Pool Leaks: Signs Types and Risks of Ignoring the Issues](https://www.gpspoolstore.com/pool-leaks-signs-types-and-risks-of-ignoring-the-issues/)

### Watching Out for Erosion

Another sign that you might have to resurface the pool has to do with erosion marks. Yes, these marks will indeed occur naturally in any environment where water is constantly moving against a particular surface. The more you use your pool, the more the water moves around, thus causing erosion marks on the sides.

However, you should be taking steps to help prevent this problem as part of your regular maintenance routine. That includes maintaining the right chemical balance. If you’re being proactive about taking care of your pool and are still experiencing erosion or other wear spots, it’s a sign that you might need to resurface in the not-too-distant future.

### Plaster Has Begun to Flake

Along the same lines, another sign that a pool probably needs to be resurfaced involves plaster that has begun to peel in certain areas. This includes not only along the steps or floor of the pool but on the walls as well.

Unfortunately, once plaster begins to flake, there isn’t really any way to repair it without resurfacing the pool itself.

### Plaster Has Begun to Lose Its Color

Similarly, if the plaster in or around your pool area has begun to show signs of discoloration, you probably need to resurface the pool. This is another phenomenon that you will likely notice at the steps of your pool first. Once the discoloration has started, it is only going to get worse until the proper resurfacing steps have been taken.

### Resurface the Pool if the Walls are Cracking

Experiencing the occasional crack is not necessarily cause for concern, especially as your pool ages. You just need to fill in those cracks while they are still in their early stages to prevent them from becoming much bigger problems down the road.

Having said that, if you get a sense that you’re filling in cracks more often than you normally do, it could be an indication that you need to resurface the pool. This is true even if the crack is still just cosmetic. If it is more severe, and it has already resulted in a leak, you should resurface the pool as soon as possible. You don’t want to run into a situation where you need to fully rebuild portions of your pool to solve the problem.

### You’re Starting to Lose Your Pebbles

![Pools tiles falling off](https://gpspoolstore.com/wp-content/uploads/pool-tiles-falling-off.jpg)Always monitor any pebbles that are attached to the surfaces in your pool. This isn’t something that you have to do every day, necessarily. But if you notice any problem areas where you’re starting to lose your pebbles, definitely keep an eye on it. Pebbles become loose over time and will eventually start to dislodge, but this shouldn’t happen for 10 or even 20 years. If it begins to happen and the problem is only getting worse, you will want to resurface your pool.

### Your Fiberglass is Failing

Finally, watch out for issues with your pool’s fiberglass overlay. Warning signs that you need to resurface the pool include an overlay that has begun to delaminate as well as situations where the water is starting to become cloudy due to chalking. When you resurface, you will also probably want to choose a different type of finish to stop this from happening again.

## Options to Resurface the Pool

These are just a few of the signs that you will need to resurface the pool on your property in the future. When you actually get to the process itself, you’ll want to consult a team of professionals like those at GPS Pools to help make sure everything goes as efficiently as possible.

The [pool resurfacing cost](https://www.gpspoolstore.com/concrete-pool-resurfacing-cost-what-to-expect/) that you’ll pay will vary depending on the finish you use. Three of the most popular options include [PebbleTec finishes](https://www.gpspoolstore.com/concrete-pool-resurfacing/pebbletec-pool-finish/), [Diamond Brite finishes](https://www.gpspoolstore.com/concrete-pool-resurfacing/diamond-brite/) and [Stonescape finishes](https://www.gpspoolstore.com/concrete-pool-resurfacing/stonescape-pool-finish/).

Marquis finishes, which are a blend of aggregated pebbles or pieces of quartz in cement that has been altered by a polymer, can last between five and 10 years with proper maintenance. Diamond pool finishes often last eight to 15 years. PebbleTec can last between 10 and 20 years.

Your resurfacing partner will help make sure you fully understand all of your choices and will also be able to perform the actual resurfacing on your behalf. After the surrounding area is properly cleared and steps are taken to protect the pool deck area, the pool will be drained and stripped. Any serious damage will be patched and repaired. After the pool’s surface has been cleaned, the existing materials will be stripped. Then, the selected finish will be applied.

We offer a wide range of [pool resurfacing](https://www.gpspoolstore.com/concrete-pool-resurfacing/) options to help customers get the most out of their pools. For years, we’ve worked hard to become an industry leader that people know they can trust, and we’re eager to show you just how true that is when it comes to resurfacing the pool you know and love.

## Keeping the Waters Perfect

Get in touch with GPS Pools today for your pool supplies, maintenance, and remodeling needs. We’re here to help you enjoy your pool to the fullest.
